title: The metallic model
summary: Metallic bonding is one of the three great bonding models, and it is the one that explains why metals behave the way they do — conducting, bending, shining, and resisting melting. This lesson builds the electron-sea model from the ground up: a lattice of cations bathed in delocalised electrons; how that single picture accounts for electrical and thermal conductivity, malleability and ductility, high melting and boiling points, and lustre; what makes one metallic bond stronger than another (cation charge, number of delocalised electrons, ionic radius); why alloying changes properties by disrupting the sliding layers; and, for HL, the trends that make transition metals distinctive. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards marks on Paper 2, so you learn the chemistry and how to keep the marks at the same time.
learningObjectives: Describe the metallic bond as the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of cations and a 'sea' of delocalised electrons.
Explain the electrical and thermal conductivity, malleability, ductility, high melting/boiling points and lustre of metals in terms of the model.
Explain how the charge of the cation, the number of delocalised electrons and the ionic radius affect metallic bond strength.
Explain why alloying changes the properties of a metal by disrupting the regular layers of the lattice.
(HL) Outline why transition metals show particularly strong metallic bonding and distinctive properties.
Present a structured explanation so that each distinct valid point earns its mark under analytic (points-based) marking with ECF.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL; transition-metal detail is HL.

simpleExplanation.title: Cations in a Sea of Electrons
simpleExplanation.summary: A metal is a giant, orderly array of positive ions sitting in a shared pool of electrons that are free to roam. Nothing owns those electrons and everything is attracted to them at once — and that one idea explains almost every property a metal has.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a car park full of parked cars (the fixed positive cations) with water flooded across the whole surface (the delocalised electrons). The water touches every car and glues the scene together, but it can flow. Tip the ground and the water carries things along (electrical current). Nudge a row of cars sideways and the water simply flows into the new gaps, so nothing shatters (malleability). Try to lift a car right out of the flood and the water pulls it back hard (high melting/boiling point). Deeper water, or cars with more charge, grip harder — that is a stronger metallic bond.
steps: Metal atoms have low ionisation energies, so each atom releases its valence electrons and becomes a positive ion (cation).
Those released electrons are delocalised — no longer tied to one atom, but free to move through the whole lattice as a 'sea'.
The metallic bond is the electrostatic attraction between the fixed cations and the mobile electron sea; it is strong and non-directional.
Read every property off this picture: mobile electrons give conductivity; a fluid sea lets layers slide (malleability); strong attraction gives high melting points.

flashcards.back: The electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) and a 'sea' of delocalised valence electrons. It is strong and non-directional.
A valence electron that is not associated with any single atom or bond, but is free to move throughout the whole metallic lattice. Only these mobile electrons carry current — the cations stay put.

The delocalised electrons are mobile; under a potential difference they drift towards the positive terminal, carrying charge. Metals conduct as solids AND when molten, because the electrons stay mobile.
The mobile delocalised electrons (and, to a lesser extent, vibrating cations) transfer kinetic energy rapidly through the lattice, so thermal energy spreads quickly.

flashcards.front: Why metals are malleable and ductile
flashcards.back: Layers of cations can slide over one another without breaking the bonding, because the electron sea flows to the new positions and keeps the attraction. Malleable = hammered into shape; ductile = drawn into wire.

flashcards.front: Why metals have high melting/boiling points
flashcards.back: A large amount of energy is needed to overcome the strong electrostatic attraction between the cations and the delocalised electrons.
The delocalised electrons at the surface absorb and re-emit photons of light across a wide range of frequencies, giving a reflective, shiny appearance.

flashcards.front: Three factors that set metallic bond strength
flashcards.back: (1) Charge on the cation — higher charge, stronger. (2) Number of delocalised electrons per atom — more, stronger. (3) Ionic radius — smaller, stronger (electrons sit closer to the nuclei).

flashcards.front: Bond-strength trend down a group (e.g. Na → K)
flashcards.back: Weaker. Same charge (+1) and one delocalised electron each, but ionic radius increases down the group, so the electron sea is further from the nuclei and less strongly held. Melting point falls.

flashcards.front: Bond-strength trend across a period (e.g. Na → Mg → Al)
flashcards.back: Stronger. Cation charge rises (+1 → +2 → +3), the number of delocalised electrons per atom rises (1 → 2 → 3) and the ionic radius falls, so the attraction grows sharply. Melting point rises.
A mixture containing at least one metal (metal + metal, or metal + non-metal). Examples: brass (Cu + Zn), steel (Fe + C), bronze (Cu + Sn).

flashcards.front: Why alloys are usually harder than the pure metal
flashcards.back: The added atoms are a different size, so they distort the regular lattice. The layers of ions can no longer slide over one another easily, so the alloy is harder and less malleable.

They can delocalise electrons from both the 4s and 3d sub-shells, contributing more delocalised electrons per atom and having relatively small ions — giving strong metallic bonds, high melting points, high density and hardness.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: metallic vs ionic bonding
flashcards.back: Ionic bonding TRANSFERS electrons, giving cations AND anions in a fixed lattice with no free electrons. Metallic bonding DELOCALISES electrons over cations only — there are no anions, and the electrons are mobile.
In malleability the LAYERS OF CATIONS slide; in conduction the DELOCALISED ELECTRONS move. Saying 'the ions carry the current' in a solid metal loses the mark.

quickCheck.prompt: In a solid metal conducting electricity, which particles actually move to carry the current?
options: The positive cations
The delocalised valence electrons
Both cations and anions
The whole layers of the lattice

quickCheck.prompt: Metals are malleable because, when a force is applied:
options: The metallic bonds break and reform as new covalent bonds
Layers of cations slide over one another while the electron sea keeps the attraction
The delocalised electrons are squeezed out of the metal
Like-charged ions align and repel, shifting the layers
quickCheck.answer: Layers of cations slide over one another while the electron sea keeps the attraction

quickCheck.prompt: Which pair correctly explains why magnesium has stronger metallic bonding than sodium?
options: Mg²⁺ has lower charge and fewer delocalised electrons
Mg²⁺ has higher charge (+2) and contributes 2 delocalised electrons per atom vs +1 and 1 for Na
Mg has a larger ionic radius than Na
Na loses more electrons than Mg
quickCheck.answer: Mg²⁺ has higher charge (+2) and contributes 2 delocalised electrons per atom vs +1 and 1 for Na

quickCheck.prompt: Going down Group 1 from Na to K, the metallic bond becomes weaker mainly because:
options: The cation charge decreases
The number of delocalised electrons per atom decreases
The ionic radius increases, so the electron sea is held less strongly
The metal becomes an alloy
quickCheck.answer: The ionic radius increases, so the electron sea is held less strongly

quickCheck.prompt: Brass (copper + zinc) is harder than pure copper because the zinc atoms:
options: Add extra delocalised electrons that lock the lattice
Are a different size and distort the layers, so they cannot slide easily
Form ionic bonds with the copper ions
Remove the delocalised electron sea
quickCheck.answer: Are a different size and distort the layers, so they cannot slide easily

faq.q: If a metal is a lattice of positive ions, why isn't it brittle like an ionic solid?
faq.a: In an ionic solid, sliding one layer brings like-charged ions next to each other; they repel strongly and the crystal shatters. In a metal the 'glue' is the non-directional electron sea. When layers of cations slide, the delocalised electrons simply flow to the new positions and the attraction is maintained — there is no layer of fixed anions to line up and repel. That is exactly why metals bend where ionic solids crack.

faq.q: Melting point or boiling point — which really measures metallic bond strength?
faq.a: Both increase with bond strength, and IB questions most often compare melting points. Strictly, boiling point is the more complete measure because boiling separates the atoms entirely and so overcomes the bonding fully, whereas melting only loosens the rigid lattice while strong attractions persist in the liquid. For comparisons within the syllabus, arguing from charge, electron count and radius will earn the marks whichever quantity is quoted.

faq.q: Why does the electron-sea model 'work' for so many different properties?
faq.a: Because two features do all the work: the electrons are MOBILE (giving conductivity, and refilling gaps so layers can slide) and the attraction is STRONG and NON-DIRECTIONAL (giving high melting points, and allowing deformation without breaking). Almost every metal property is one of those two ideas in disguise, which is why examiners reward answers that name the feature and then link it to the property.

faq.q: (HL) What makes transition metals special within the model?
faq.a: Transition metals can involve electrons from both the 3d and 4s sub-shells in the delocalised sea, so they typically contribute more delocalised electrons per atom than a Group 1 or 2 metal, and their ions are relatively small. More electrons plus small, highly charged cations means stronger metallic bonding — which shows up as high melting points, high density, hardness and strength. This 3d/4s involvement is HL-only detail.

sections.content: Wires that carry current, beams that hold up buildings, foil you can crumple in one hand, a spoon that warms in hot soup — all of these come from a single bonding model. Metals are held together by the metallic bond, and the picture behind it is deceptively simple: a regular lattice of positive ions sitting in a shared 'sea' of delocalised electrons. In this lesson we build that model carefully, then read every characteristic property straight off it, work out what makes one metallic bond stronger than another, and see why mixing metals into alloys changes how they behave. As in every lesson, the worked examples are marked exactly the way our engine marks your own Paper 2 answers — point by point — so you learn the chemistry and how to keep the marks together.

Metal atoms sit on the left of the periodic table and have low ionisation energies, so they lose their outer (valence) electrons readily. In the solid, each atom gives up those electrons and becomes a positive ion, a cation. The released electrons are no longer attached to any one atom: they are delocalised, free to move throughout the entire piece of metal, forming a 'sea' of negative charge that washes over and between the fixed cations. The metallic bond is the strong, non-directional electrostatic attraction between this sea of delocalised electrons and the lattice of cations. Because the attraction is non-directional — pulling equally in all directions rather than along fixed bonds — the structure can be deformed without breaking, a point we will return to for malleability.

items: Metal atoms lose valence electrons to become a lattice of positive ions (cations).
The lost electrons are delocalised, forming a mobile 'sea of electrons'.
The metallic bond is the electrostatic attraction between the cations and the delocalised electrons.
The bond is strong and NON-DIRECTIONAL — this is what lets metals deform without shattering.

sections.content: Reading the properties off the model

Almost every characteristic property of a metal is one of two features of the model in disguise: the electrons are MOBILE, and the attraction is STRONG. Name the feature, then link it to the property — that is the structure examiners reward.

items: **Electrical conductivity.** The delocalised electrons are mobile. Under a potential difference they drift towards the positive terminal, carrying charge as a current. Metals conduct as solids and when molten, because the electron sea stays mobile in both states.
**Thermal conductivity.** The same mobile electrons transfer kinetic energy rapidly through the lattice (the vibrating cations help too), so heat spreads quickly.

**Malleability and ductility.** When a force is applied, layers of cations slide over one another. The electron sea immediately flows to the new positions, keeping the attraction, so the metal changes shape without shattering — hammered into sheets (malleable) or drawn into wire (ductile).

**High melting and boiling points.** The electrostatic attraction between cations and the electron sea is strong, so a large amount of energy is needed to break the lattice apart. Most metals are solids with high melting points at room temperature.
**Lustre.** Surface delocalised electrons absorb and re-emit photons across many frequencies, so a fresh metal surface is shiny and reflective.

sections.question: Explain, in terms of the metallic bonding model, why copper is used for electrical wiring and can also be bent into shape without snapping. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — read straight off the electron-sea picture.**

Conductivity:
1. Copper consists of a lattice of Cu cations surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons. [1]
2. These delocalised electrons are mobile, so when a potential difference is applied they drift through the metal and carry an electric current. [1]

Bending without snapping (malleability):
3. When a force is applied, the layers of cations slide over one another. [1]
4. The delocalised electrons flow to the new positions and maintain the electrostatic attraction (the bonding is non-directional), so the structure deforms rather than shatters. [1]

*Each of the four sentences makes one distinct, creditable point: identify the structure, link mobile electrons to current, describe layers sliding, and explain why sliding does not break the metal.*

sections.content: For 'explain a property' questions, use a two-move sentence every time: name the feature of the model, then link it to the property. 'The electrons are delocalised and mobile (feature), so they carry charge when a voltage is applied (property).' Vague answers like 'metals have free electrons so they conduct' often earn one mark where a two-move answer earns two.
What makes one metallic bond stronger than another

Not all metallic bonds are equal, and comparing them is a favourite exam task. Three factors decide the strength of the attraction between the cations and the electron sea. First, the charge on the cation: a more highly charged cation attracts the electron sea more strongly. Second, the number of delocalised electrons contributed per atom: more electrons in the sea means more attraction. Third, the ionic radius: a smaller cation lets the electron sea sit closer to the positive nuclei, strengthening the bond. Strong metallic bonding shows up as high melting and boiling points, high density and greater hardness.

items: **Charge of the cation** — higher charge (e.g. Mg²⁺ vs Na⁺) means stronger attraction.
**Number of delocalised electrons per atom** — more electrons in the sea (e.g. 2 for Mg vs 1 for Na) means stronger bonding.
**Ionic radius** — a smaller cation holds the electron sea closer to the nuclei, strengthening the bond.
**Down a group** (Na → K): radius increases, so bonding weakens and melting point falls.
**Across a period** (Na → Mg → Al): charge and electron count rise while radius falls, so bonding strengthens sharply and melting point rises.

sections.question: Sodium melts at 98 °C and magnesium at 650 °C. Explain, in terms of metallic bonding, why magnesium has the much higher melting point. [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — compare the two structures point by point.**

1. In sodium each atom loses one electron to form Na⁺ and contributes one delocalised electron; in magnesium each atom loses two to form Mg²⁺ and contributes two delocalised electrons. [1]
2. The Mg²⁺ ion has a greater charge (+2 vs +1) and a smaller ionic radius than Na⁺, and there are more delocalised electrons per atom in magnesium. [1]
3. Therefore the electrostatic attraction between the cations and the electron sea is stronger in magnesium, so more energy is needed to break the lattice apart — giving the higher melting point. [1]

*A complete answer names at least one factor (charge, electron count, or radius), links it to a stronger attraction, and connects that to more energy / a higher melting point. Mentioning more than one factor gives extra security if one point is not accepted.*

sections.content: An alloy is a mixture that contains at least one metal — either two or more metals, or a metal with a non-metal. Alloys are made because they usually improve on the pure metal: harder, stronger, or more corrosion-resistant. Brass is copper mixed with zinc; steel is iron with a little carbon; bronze is copper with tin. The reason alloying works comes straight from the sliding-layers picture. In a pure metal every ion is the same size, so the layers are neat and slide over each other relatively easily. Introduce atoms of a different size and the regular layers are distorted; the layers can no longer slide past one another smoothly, so the alloy resists deformation — it is harder and less malleable than the pure metal.

items: An alloy is a mixture containing at least one metal.
Alloys are usually harder and stronger than the pure metal.
Atoms of a different size distort the regular lattice layers.
Distorted layers cannot slide over each other easily, so the alloy resists being deformed.

sections.question: Brass is an alloy of copper and zinc. Explain, in terms of structure, why brass is harder than pure copper. [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

1. In pure copper the cations are all the same size and form regular layers that can slide over one another fairly easily (so pure copper is soft/malleable). [1]
2. In brass, zinc atoms of a different size are present in the lattice, distorting the regular arrangement of the layers. [1]
3. The distorted layers can no longer slide past one another easily, so brass is harder and stronger than pure copper. [1]

*The three points are: describe the pure metal, state that different-sized atoms distort the layers, and conclude that harder sliding means a harder metal.*

sections.content: Transition metals sit at the strong end of the metallic-bonding scale, and this extra detail is HL-only. Unlike a Group 1 or Group 2 metal, a transition metal can delocalise electrons from both the 4s and the inner 3d sub-shell, so it typically contributes more delocalised electrons per atom to the sea. Combined with relatively small ions, this gives especially strong metallic bonding. The consequences are the properties transition metals are known for: high melting and boiling points, high density, and considerable hardness and strength — which is why iron, chromium, titanium and their alloys dominate structural and engineering uses. (SL students are responsible for the model and the three strength factors, but not this 3d/4s explanation.)

items: Transition metals can delocalise both 4s and 3d electrons, giving more delocalised electrons per atom.
Their ions are relatively small, so the electron sea is held close and strongly.
Result: strong metallic bonding → high melting/boiling points, high density, hardness and strength.
This 3d/4s reasoning is HL only; the general model and strength factors are common to SL and HL.
**Saying the ions carry the current** — in a solid metal it is the DELOCALISED ELECTRONS that move and carry charge; the cations stay fixed in the lattice.
**Confusing metallic with ionic bonding** — metallic bonding has cations ONLY, delocalised over a mobile electron sea; there are no anions and no transferred electrons sitting on a partner ion.
**Explaining malleability by 'the bonds break'** — the metallic bond does NOT break; layers of cations SLIDE while the electron sea flows and keeps the attraction.
**Comparing bond strength with only one idea** — a full comparison should reference the cation charge, the number of delocalised electrons and/or the ionic radius, then link to a stronger attraction.

**Forgetting that stronger attraction ⇒ more energy ⇒ higher melting point** — state the whole chain; stopping at 'stronger bonds' without linking to energy or melting point can drop the conclusion mark.
**Describing an alloy as adding electrons or forming new bonds** — alloying works by DISTORTING the layers with different-sized atoms so they cannot slide, not by changing the bonding type.
**Using 3d/4s transition-metal reasoning in an SL answer as if required** — it is correct chemistry but HL-only detail; SL answers are marked on the model and the three strength factors.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

On Paper 2 an 'explain' question like this is marked analytically (points-based): each distinct, valid chemical point is worth one mark, and the marks are independent of one another. There is no single 'right wording' — the engine credits any equivalent statement that makes the point, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means a reasoning mark you have earned is not taken away because a later conclusion slips. In a structured answer, method/reasoning marks (M) are earned for correct explanatory steps and answer/conclusion marks (A) for the final linked statement. Study how each mark below is tied to one specific point.

sections.question: Explain, in terms of metallic bonding, why magnesium has a higher melting point than sodium and why both conduct electricity. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full response.**

*Higher melting point of magnesium:*
Magnesium atoms each release two valence electrons to form Mg²⁺, while sodium atoms release one to form Na⁺. Magnesium therefore has cations of higher charge (+2 vs +1), contributes two delocalised electrons per atom rather than one, and has a smaller ionic radius. The electrostatic attraction between the cations and the delocalised electron sea is consequently stronger in magnesium, so more energy is required to break the lattice apart and the melting point is higher.

*Why both conduct electricity:*
Both metals contain delocalised electrons that are free to move; when a potential difference is applied these mobile electrons drift through the lattice and carry an electric current.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — the structural difference.** Awarded for stating that Mg forms Mg²⁺ / contributes 2 delocalised electrons while Na forms Na⁺ / contributes 1 (equivalently: higher cation charge and/or more delocalised electrons and/or smaller radius for Mg). Any ONE valid strength factor, correctly stated, earns this reasoning mark.
- **A1 — the melting-point conclusion.** Awarded for linking that to a STRONGER attraction between cations and the electron sea, needing MORE energy to overcome, hence a higher melting point. This is the conclusion mark — it must connect the reasoning to the property. **ECF:** if a student named a factor slightly loosely in M1 but correctly argued 'stronger attraction → more energy → higher melting point', A1 is still awarded on their reasoning.
- **M1 — the conduction mechanism.** Awarded for identifying that the delocalised electrons are MOBILE / free to move. This is the reasoning half of the conduction answer and stands alone.
- **A1 — the conduction conclusion.** Awarded for stating that, under a potential difference, these mobile electrons move/drift and carry the current. Naming the moving particle as the electron (not the ion) is essential here.

**'Accept equivalents; each distinct point = 1 mark.'** The engine accepts any wording that makes the point: 'sea of electrons', 'delocalised electrons', 'free electrons' all score for the mobile-electron idea; 'greater nuclear charge on the cation', 'ion has a 2+ charge' and 'more electrons in the sea' are all accepted routes to M1. Points are marked independently, so a strong conduction answer still scores even if the melting-point comparison is weak, and vice versa.

**Bottom line:** four independent points, marked point by point. A student who writes one merged sentence ('Mg bonds are stronger and metals conduct') risks scoring 1–2 of 4; a student who makes each distinct point — structural difference, stronger-attraction conclusion, mobile electrons, current carried — banks all four, and ECF protects the reasoning even if a detail slips.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — analytically, one mark per distinct valid point, accepting equivalents, with ECF on your reasoning. So make each point separately and explicitly: name the strength factor, state 'stronger attraction, more energy, higher melting point', and say clearly that the DELOCALISED ELECTRONS move to carry current. Merging everything into one sentence is what quietly loses marks the engine would otherwise award.

The metallic model completes the trio of bonding types — ionic, covalent and metallic — and the same habit of thought runs through all of them: describe the particles and the forces between them, then read the properties off that picture. In later topics the bonding continuum (S2.4) places metallic bonding alongside ionic and covalent on a triangle of bonding character, and the reactivity of metals in redox and electrochemistry (Reactivity 3) builds directly on how readily metals lose those valence electrons in the first place. Own the electron-sea model here and those topics become extensions of a picture you already carry.
